title: Executive Order 9508—Authorizing the Secretary of War to Take Possession of and to Operate Certain Plants and Facilities of Montgomery Ward & Co., Incorporated

flags.justification: Broad operational authority ('any manner that he deems essential'), though the seizure itself is triggered by, and bounded by, enforcing specific already-adjudicated National War Labor Board directive orders (Sec. 1-2) rather than open policy discretion.

The action is grounded in and bounded by an existing, already-completed NWLB adjudication process (directive orders with specific dates), plus the standard 60-day cap (Sec. 4) and a wage-shortfall reporting obligation to the President (Sec. 2) — among the most procedurally grounded seizure orders in this corpus.
Spans multiple facilities across several cities, but all are operations of one company subject to one coherent enforcement action, not a bundling of distinct issues.
Sec. 2 requires observing specific, named NWLB directive orders by exact date — concrete, not open-ended.
No incentive structure rewarding behavior contrary to the order's purpose is identifiable.
Sec. 4's 60-day cap is a genuine, explicit termination trigger.
This order reinforces rather than bypasses an existing oversight process — it enforces compliance with an NWLB adjudication the company had already contested and lost.

The seizure's entire justification rests on an independent, quasi-judicial NWLB determination that already occurred, plus the 60-day cap — multiple real external checks are built into the order's own design and justification.

Uses federal seizure power specifically as an enforcement mechanism against a private company's defiance of an administrative board's directive orders, functionally giving NWLB determinations coercive force via the threat of seizure — a notable precedent for using this authority to compel compliance with agency adjudications rather than merely to respond to production disruption.
Sec. 3's cooperation directive to named agencies is coordination, not mandate redirection.
Sec. 5's broad definition of covered 'plants and facilities' expands rather than narrows coverage — the opposite of an exemption.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 2/20 = 10.0%. The historically most famous and controversial seizure order in this corpus, yet structurally one of the lowest-scoring — it enforces an already-adjudicated determination rather than exercising open-ended judgment, a clean illustration that structural weight and historical/political salience are distinct measures.
